investor.name: Parth Chopra
investor.about: Product builder turned investor. Co-founded Laguna (YC W20, fintech). Early employee and Product Lead at OpenSea (30 to 300+ employees, $10B+ GMV). Joined Bain Capital Crypto in 2023. Former Sequoia Capital Scout. Co-built VC Whisper with Tyler Richards: an AI tool that analyzes pitch transcripts and generates investor briefings for founders. Writes on Substack about venture, public markets, and crypto.
investor.stageFocus: Pre-seed / Seed (angel); Series A+ (Bain Capital Crypto)

company.name: Dazzle
company.description: AI-native live TV platform. Dazzle generates continuous, original video content in real time using AI video models (LTX-2 diffusion transformer on GPU), driven by an autonomous AI agent loop that writes scripts, generates scenes with images/video/speech in parallel, and streams the result as a live channel. Channels are programmable via MCP: any external AI agent can direct what appears on screen through 17 tools (content, creative direction, style, chat, HUD). Target markets are creators and streamers (24/7 channels without human production), AI developers and agent builders (visual output channel for agents), and brands (always-fresh ambient content for digital signage). Neuro-sama ($400K+/month, one AI character) proves consumer appetite. Dazzle is the platform that makes that accessible to anyone.

fitScore.explanation: Parth is not a traditional VC for this conversation. He is a builder who co-created VC Whisper, showing he ships AI products as side projects. The fit is strongest as an advisor or angel. His OpenSea experience scaling a marketplace from 30 to 300+ people gives him direct pattern-matching for Dazzle's inflection point. His personal interest in creative, unconventional platforms (keik.org) and his builder instinct align well with Dazzle's category-creating position. The crypto focus at Bain Capital is tangential to Dazzle's core thesis (AI video generation, creator economy, MCP ecosystem), but his personal interests and angel capacity make him a high-signal connection.
fitScore.thesisAlignment: Moderate on institutional thesis, strong on personal interests. Parth tweets about creative platforms that break conventions. He co-built VC Whisper, showing he ships in the AI tooling space. His Substack thesis about 'baby businesses that everyone prices like they're all grown up' maps to Dazzle: a small team building an AI TV network that looks like a streaming toy but is actually a platform for agent-driven live video generation. The MCP programmability layer is the kind of infrastructure play he understands from OpenSea's protocol work.
fitScore.portfolioPatternMatch: Indirect. Bain Capital Crypto invests in decentralized infrastructure, not AI video generation. But Parth's personal pattern is more relevant: OpenSea was a marketplace on top of a protocol layer (Ethereum/Seaport), and Dazzle is a platform on top of AI infrastructure (LTX-2, MCP). Both are protocol-native products that create new categories rather than competing in existing ones.
fitScore.stageFit: Good for angel. His scout range was $25K-$100K. Dazzle seeking advisory/angel fits within his personal investment capacity, not Bain Capital Crypto's institutional checks.
fitScore.checkSizeFit: Angel/advisory conversation, not an institutional raise. His scout-era sweet spot of $50K fits a small angel round.

career.note: Evaluates and leads investments in crypto infrastructure. Bain Capital Crypto believes the internet is shifting toward open, community-driven, decentralized services.

Built with Tyler Richards. AI tool that analyzes pitch transcripts (what landed, who to talk to next) and generates pre-meeting investor briefings. The 'Prepare' feature is conceptually identical to this briefing.

Sole PM at hire. Scaled from 30 to 300+ employees, $10B+ GMV. Led payments ($100M+ GMV), chain expansion (6+ chains), API (self-serve, 10% of daily GMV), and protocol (Seaport). Wrote extensively about the experience on Substack.
Angel investments through Sequoia's scout program. $25K-$100K checks, sweet spot $50K. Web3/crypto focus at pre-seed and seed.

Fintech startup. Started as a way to buy real estate together, pivoted to decentralized hedge funds with paying financial content creators. Team of five. Did not find PMF but learned deeply about building. Now inactive.
First career role. Product management at scale.

education.degree: BS Computer Science (Penn Engineering) + BS Economics (Wharton)
education.institution: University of Pennsylvania (Jerome Fisher M&T Program)

press.title: Fireside Chat: On Investing & Building in Crypto & Tech
press.summary: Spoke to 31 attendees at Penn about crypto venture capital, founding blockchain-adjacent companies, upcoming ecosystem trends, and post-graduation career paths. Offered 1:1 coffee chats the next day.

press.title: What I learned working at a high-growth crypto startup
press.summary: Detailed lessons from OpenSea: operating with imperfect information, embracing constant change, building domain expertise, demonstrating progress through tangible proof points. Warned against complacency during hypergrowth. Said scaling successfully rivals finding PMF in difficulty.

press.title: Venture in Public Markets
press.summary: Core thesis: investing in early-stage ventures and public 'longs' share surprising similarities. Introduced the 'card flip' framework and the idea of 'baby businesses that everyone prices like they're all grown up.' Identified a structural gap in the 18-month to 4-year horizon where neither hedge funds nor index funds compete.

relevantContent.text: there's a new japanese social media site keik dot org -it records and shares you typing your posts -you lose 'points' when you scroll, gain when you post -on a person's profile you see a LIVE video of them using the app its so deranged & creative, completely cracks me up
relevantContent.relevance: Parth is genuinely excited by creative, unconventional platforms that reimagine what live content looks like. He called keik.org 'deranged & creative' as a compliment. Dazzle is that same energy applied to video: instead of a person streaming, an AI agent generates continuous original video in real time. The platform creates a new category the way keik.org does. Lead with what makes Dazzle weird and unprecedented, not what makes it safe.

relevantContent.text: Links are the internet's love language.
relevantContent.relevance: Parth thinks about the internet's primitives. Dazzle builds on a new primitive: MCP (Model Context Protocol), the open standard that lets any AI agent call tools. Dazzle exposes 17 MCP tools so any agent can direct a live video channel. This maps to his mental model of protocol-layer products from OpenSea (Seaport protocol) and Bain Capital Crypto (open infrastructure).

relevantContent.text: The fundamental skill involves identifying baby businesses that everyone is pricing like they're all grown up, then betting on meaningful transformation before recognition arrives.
relevantContent.relevance: Dazzle fits this framing precisely. It looks like 'an AI streaming toy' but is actually an AI-native TV network with an MCP API that any agent can program. Neuro-sama (one AI character, $400K+/month in Twitch subs) proves the economics. Dazzle abstracts that into a platform anyone can use. Parth's framework rewards exactly this kind of underestimation.

recentTweets.text: there's a new japanese social media site keik dot org -it records and shares you typing your posts -you lose 'points' when you scroll, gain when you post -on a person's profile you see a LIVE video of them using the app its so deranged & creative, completely cracks me up
Links are the internet's love language.

gamePlan.howTheyThink: Parth is a builder first, investor second. He co-founded a startup (Laguna), was sole PM at one of the fastest-growing marketplaces ever (OpenSea), and still ships side projects (VC Whisper). He evaluates through the lens of 'could I have built this?' and 'does this team understand something others don't?' His Substack reveals a pattern-matching mind: he looks for category mispricings, where everyone thinks something is one thing but it's actually another. At OpenSea, he learned that deep domain expertise compounds and that obsessive product usage (CEO Devin used the product constantly) beats secondhand reports. He'll want to see Dazzle running live. His personal interests skew toward creative, unconventional platforms (keik.org tweet) and protocol-layer infrastructure (OpenSea Seaport, Bain Capital Crypto). He values speed and tangible proof over polished decks.

connections.title: He built the other side of this product
connections.detail: Parth co-created VC Whisper, which includes a 'Prepare' feature that generates investor briefings before meetings. He understands the problem space viscerally. This briefing is being generated by a tool in the same category. The meta-demo writes itself: 'I used something like what you built to prepare for this conversation.'

connections.title: Platform on a protocol layer
connections.detail: Parth spent two years building on the protocol layer at OpenSea (Seaport) and now invests in decentralized infrastructure at Bain Capital Crypto. Dazzle is built on MCP, the open protocol that lets any AI agent call tools. The 17-tool MCP API means Dazzle channels are programmable by any agent, the same way OpenSea was programmable by any wallet. He'll immediately understand why building on an open protocol creates network effects.

connections.title: Scaling a marketplace from zero
connections.detail: Parth joined OpenSea at 30 people and watched it scale to 300+ with $10B+ GMV. Dazzle is at a similar inflection point: working product, real-time AI video generation pipeline, MCP API, billing via Stripe, and a clear multi-sided market (creators, developers, viewers). His OpenSea lessons (hire sparingly, obsessive product usage, avoid complacency) are directly applicable.

connections.title: Creative platforms that break conventions
connections.detail: His keik.org tweet shows genuine excitement for platforms that reimagine what content looks like. Dazzle is that for live video: instead of a human streaming or a pre-recorded loop replaying, an AI agent generates continuous original scenes in real time. It is a new kind of content that did not exist before. He won't need convincing that the category can exist; he's already looking for it.

connections.title: Neuro-sama economics as proof point
connections.detail: Parth's Substack thesis about 'baby businesses everyone prices like they're all grown up' maps perfectly. Neuro-sama, a single AI character on Twitch built by one developer, generates $400K+/month in subscription revenue. Dazzle is the platform that makes that kind of AI channel accessible to anyone. The unit economics are already proven by the market; Dazzle is the infrastructure layer.

predictedQuestions.question: How is this different from Runway, Sora, or other AI video generation tools?
predictedQuestions.suggestedApproach: Those tools generate discrete clips on demand: you submit a prompt, wait 90 seconds to 5 minutes, and download a 5-25 second video. Dazzle generates continuously as a live stream. An AI agent loop writes scripts, generates scenes (image/video/speech in parallel via LTX-2), and streams the output as a live channel. It is the difference between a photo printer and a TV station. Show him a running channel.

predictedQuestions.question: How do you make money on this?
predictedQuestions.suggestedApproach: Credit-based billing via Stripe, already implemented. Users buy credits, streaming consumes credits per GPU-minute. Three revenue surfaces: creators paying for 24/7 channels (replacing $50-125/month in fragmented tools), developers paying for MCP API access to give their agents visual output, and brands paying per-screen-per-month for always-fresh ambient content (replacing $2,000-10,000/quarter agency production cycles). Neuro-sama proves the creator tier alone can support $400K+/month per channel.

predictedQuestions.question: What's the moat? Can't anyone wrap an AI video model in a streaming layer?
predictedQuestions.suggestedApproach: The generation pipeline is the hard part. Each step involves plan generation, parallel script/scene/asset/speech production, speculative pre-generation of branching choices, real-time streaming, and an autonomous agent loop that maintains narrative coherence across hours of content. The MCP API with 17 tools, the agent lifecycle management, and the real-time event system (content_low, chat_activity, billing) create a platform other agents build on. This is not a wrapper; it is an operating system for AI-driven live video.

predictedQuestions.question: Who are the first customers?
predictedQuestions.suggestedApproach: Three wedges. First: AI developers and agent builders in the MCP ecosystem (50,000-200,000 globally). Dazzle is the only MCP server that gives an agent a live visual output channel. Second: lofi/ambient creators and 24/7 streamers who currently spend 8-12 hours per cycle rebuilding content loops. Third: Twitch streamers whose channels are dark 18+ hours/day. The developer wedge is the GTM priority because developers are both customers and distribution (they build agents that pull consumer adoption behind them).

avoid.topic: Don't pitch Dazzle as 'just for streamers'
avoid.reason: Parth has deep marketplace and protocol experience. He'll pattern-match 'streaming tool' to commodity and think small. Dazzle is a platform for agent-driven live video with an MCP API. The streaming channels are one surface; the programmable infrastructure is the business. Lead with the platform, not the use case.

avoid.topic: Don't oversell crypto or web3 overlap
avoid.reason: Just because Parth works in crypto does not make Dazzle a crypto product. The MCP protocol is open infrastructure, not blockchain infrastructure. He'll see through forced framing. Let him draw the parallel between open protocols (MCP) and decentralized infrastructure himself.

avoid.topic: Don't compare to Neuro-sama without nuance
avoid.reason: Neuro-sama is a talking head (LLM + Live2D avatar). Dazzle generates actual video, not avatar animation. The economics are comparable ($400K+/month proves consumer appetite for AI-native channels), but the technology is fundamentally different. Make the distinction: Dazzle generates what is shown, not just what is said.

otherInvestors.name: Tyler Richards
otherInvestors.why: Co-built VC Whisper with Parth. If the conversation with Parth goes well, Tyler is the natural second connection. He's a builder in the AI tooling space and would understand Dazzle's technical approach to MCP-programmable live video.
otherInvestors.talkingPoint: Parth and Tyler built VC Whisper together. Ask Parth for an intro to Tyler if the conversation resonates. Tyler would immediately understand the MCP API angle.

otherInvestors.name: Sarah Guo
otherInvestors.why: Conviction is an AI-native fund. Sarah has been vocal about the 'AI-first media' thesis and invests in infrastructure that enables new categories of AI-generated content. Dazzle's position as an AI-native TV platform fits her portfolio pattern. Conviction's check sizes ($1-10M) would fit a seed round.
otherInvestors.talkingPoint: Dazzle is the first platform where AI agents can direct live video channels via MCP. Neuro-sama proves $400K+/month unit economics for AI-native content. Dazzle makes that a platform.

otherInvestors.name: Justine Moore
otherInvestors.why: Covers creator economy and consumer social at a16z. Would understand Dazzle's bet on AI-generated live content replacing manual production workflows for creators. The 24/7 streaming market (Lofi Girl at $3.5M/year) demonstrates the economics she looks for.
otherInvestors.talkingPoint: Creators spend $50-125/month on fragmented tools to maintain 24/7 channels. Dazzle collapses that into one platform with AI generation. The creator economy AI tools market is $4.35B and growing at 31% CAGR.

otherInvestors.name: Elad Gil
otherInvestors.why: Prolific angel in AI infrastructure. Has invested in multiple AI model companies and platforms. Would understand the technical depth of Dazzle's generation pipeline (LTX-2, speculative generation, MCP API) and the platform potential of agent-programmable live video.
otherInvestors.talkingPoint: Every AI video tool generates clips. Dazzle generates continuously. The MCP API means any AI agent can direct a live channel, creating a new distribution vector through the developer ecosystem.

quickReference.oneLinerThesis: Builder first, investor second. Looks for products that seem small but are actually platform-scale. Loves creative platforms that break conventions and protocol-layer infrastructure.
quickReference.oneLinerStyle: Hands-on builder who ships side projects (VC Whisper). Evaluates through 'could I have built this?' Not a deck person; wants to see the product running live.
topQuestions: How is this different from Runway/Sora?
What's the moat beyond wrapping a video model?
proofPoints: 17-tool MCP API: any agent can direct a live channel
Real-time generation pipeline: LTX-2 video, parallel scene/speech/asset production
quickReference.doNotSay: Don't call it a 'streaming tool.' Don't force the crypto angle. Don't compare to Neuro-sama without distinguishing generated video from avatar animation.
